fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need to build out the mobile analytics tracking client SDK for android. basically devs are complaining that they have to hand-roll all the request stuff themselves and it keeps breaking in weird ways — wrong decimal formats for prices, malformed JSON for cart items, that kind of thing. we need a clean library that handles all of this for them.

the main things we need: some kind of price helper (devs keep getting the decimal formatting wrong), a checksum utility (similar to what we did for the file verification flow in that other module, you know the one), custom metadata support with both the indexed key-value style and the flat dimension fields, a cart/order serializer, a parameter builder with that 'first write wins' mode people keep asking for, URL encoding for the query strings, and a batching wrapper for sending multiple events at once.

one thing that keeps coming up in bug reports — the batching has a page size limit that devs aren't aware of, and the null/empty handling on dimensions and variables is inconsistent across implementations. also the checksum thing should degrade gracefully instead of blowing up.

we should follow the same repo structure pattern as before — nothing too over-engineered but also not one giant file. tests should be runnable with a single bash command and output files should be namespaced properly so different test runs don't stomp on each other. ping me if you need the exact wire format specs, i can dig them up.

quick follow-up after the team questions came in: for prices, we should make this super boring and consistent. the input is always an integer representing minor units (cents), divided by 100, and we always render with exactly two fractional digits using a dot (.) as the decimal separator, regardless of the input value. so 1 cent → 0.01 and 1000 cents → 10.00. no locale weirdness, no dropping trailing zeroes.

on the checksum side, the graceful fallback is important. when the input cannot produce a valid checksum, like null byte arrays, directories, or non-hashable content, we output the literal string 'null' instead of throwing an exception or bubbling an error up through the adapter. only text-mode inputs with a valid string value should produce a 'checksum=<UPPERCASE_MD5>' line. this is Feature 2 (md5_checksum): the checksum utility that computes uppercase MD5 digests for text inputs and returns the literal string 'null' for inputs that cannot be hashed, defined in feature2_checksum.json.

for custom variables, only positive (greater than zero) integer indexes are valid. negative indexes and zero are silently ignored, no error, just skip the entry. if the dev passes array-form entries, they must have exactly two elements; arrays of any other length are also ignored. and if everything gets filtered out and there’s nothing left, the rendered result needs to be 'custom_variables_json=null', not an empty JSON object '{}'.

for custom dimensions, same general spirit: index zero is always ignored. empty string values are ignored and not stored. setting an existing dimension to null removes it from the collection. values longer than the accepted maximum are truncated before output. only positive indexes with non-empty, non-null values produce output fields named 'dimension<index>'.

also, on structure, yes, this really does need to stay split up. we’ve got multiple distinct responsibilities here — formatting, checksums, serialization, parameter building, URL encoding, bulk dispatch — so this must NOT be a single god file. it should use a multi-file directory structure like src/ and tests/, with the core domain logic separated from the I/O adapter. the execution adapter reads JSON from stdin and delegates to the core, and those pieces need to be physically or at minimum logically separated. that matches the Scale-Driven Code Organization constraint in the PRD: a multi-file repository with src/ and tests/ directories separating core domain logic, the execution adapter, and the test harness.

trigger_keywords: bulk event page size limit
how many events per batch page
batching maximum events per request
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each bulk dispatch page holds a maximum of 20 events. If the event list is empty, no page entries are produced and the iterator reports has_next=false and next_after_end=null. The JSON payload for each page includes a 'requests' array and an optional 'token_auth' field when a token is provided.

trigger_keywords: try_set first write wins semantics
parameter not overwritten when already set
preserve first value tracking parameter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'try_set' method stores a value only when the parameter is not already present. Subsequent try_set calls for the same parameter key are silently ignored. The 'set' method always overwrites, and setting a value to null or empty string removes the parameter entirely.

trigger_keywords: query encoding format url percent encoding
how is the query string prefixed
URL encoding reserved characters
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The encoded query string must start with a '?' character, use the public parameter wire keys, and percent-encode reserved characters following UTF-8 rules. The output line is formatted as 'encoded_query=?<key>=<value>&...'.

trigger_keywords: test output file naming convention
how are test result files named
stdout file path format for test cases
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each test case writes its raw stdout to 'rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt'. For example, the first case (index 0) in 'feature1_price_formatting.json' run with '--cases-dir public_test_cases' produces 'rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_price_formatting@000.txt'. Output is namespaced by cases-dir so different runs never overwrite each other.

trigger_keywords: ecommerce item array structure
what fields go into item serialization
order of fields in ecommerce item json
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each ecommerce item is serialized as a JSON array beginning with 'sku', followed by optional 'name', 'category', price formatted from cents (two decimal places), and 'quantity' when present. The 'remove' action deletes an item by SKU, 'clear' removes all items. An empty collection renders as 'items_json=[]'.

trigger_keywords: copy from base parameter semantics
how does base request copying work
inherit parameters from base tracking request
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When 'copy_from_base' is true, base operations are first applied to a separate source request object, and then all resulting parameters are copied into the current request before the current operations are applied. This allows inheriting a baseline set of parameters from a prior request state.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The adapter routes by the top-level feature field for most cases: price_formatting, md5_checksum, custom_variables, custom_dimensions, ecommerce_items, track_parameters, query_encoding, and bulk_wrapper. The action values summary and api_url are only sub-actions used within track_parameters-style cases, not the full dispatcher vocabulary.

trigger_keywords: matomo feature dispatcher
track_parameters actions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The top-level dispatcher field is feature. Valid feature values include price_formatting, md5_checksum, custom_variables, custom_dimensions, ecommerce_items, track_parameters, query_encoding, and bulk_wrapper. For feature=track_parameters, action may be summary or api_url.

how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: tests should be runnable with a single bash command and output files should be namespaced properly so different test runs don't stomp on each other
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The test harness entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' with '--cases-dir <subdir>' support, writing output to 'rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt'

context_pointers.vague_reference: that 'first write wins' mode people keep asking for
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The 'try_set' method on the tracking parameter builder (Feature 6), which stores a value only when the parameter key is not already present in the map

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have the exact spec detail handy for that one — can you check the PRD or the test case files in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the precise behavior? The test cases are the source of truth for wire format and edge case handling.

feature1_price_formatting.json.description: Formats integer minor-unit amounts into two-decimal major-unit monetary strings using a dot decimal separator.

cases.expected_output:
amount_cents=1000
price=10.00
amount_cents=3950
price=39.50
amount_cents=1
price=0.01
amount_cents=25034
price=250.34

feature2_checksum.json.description: Calculates uppercase MD5 checksums for text-like inputs and returns null for inputs that cannot produce a checksum.

feature3_custom_variables.json.description: Builds JSON for indexed custom name/value pairs, preserving valid inherited entries, escaping JSON characters, trimming overlong text, and ignoring invalid indexes or malformed value arrays.

feature4_custom_dimensions.json.description: Converts positive, non-empty custom dimension values into numbered output fields, removes null values, ignores zero indexes, and trims overlong values.

feature5_ecommerce_items.json.description: Serializes ecommerce item collections as JSON arrays, formatting prices from cents and supporting item removal and clearing.

feature6_track_parameters.json.description: Stores tracking query parameters as strings, removes parameters set to null or empty strings, preserves first values when requested, and can copy all existing parameters from a base request.

feature7_query_encoding.json.description: Encodes tracking parameters into a URL query string using the public wire keys and UTF-8 escaping.

feature8_bulk_event_wrapping.json.description: Groups event query strings into bulk request pages, preserves the base API URL, includes an optional authentication token in JSON payloads, and returns a direct event URL for single-event dispatch.
